column15: The chatbot seemed to go through an iterative process in which it refined its answers as it "thought." It typically started at the beginning and would refine the words one at a time until the very end when a larger group of words would be resolved all at once. With this being said, the changes were not completely linear and the model did go back and change its word choices with relative frequency while it was "thinking."

column27: The main differences I noticed were in detail, accuracy, and clarity. One chatbot gave shorter, simpler answers that were sometimes a bit vague or had small mistakes, while the other provided more detailed and thorough explanations with examples and context. The more detailed chatbot felt more confident and trustworthy, but it also occasionally included extra information that wasn’t completely accurate.

column15: The chatbot appears to have analyzed each question by identifying key terms and matching them to information patterns it learned during training. For practical questions, it generated clear, actionable suggestions by drawing on commonly recommended solutions. For factual questions, it relied on stored knowledge and associations to provide direct answers. When classifying items, it compared each entry against known categories and selected those that fit the criteria.
column16: It likely performed this way because it is optimized to prioritize clarity, structure, and instruction-following. Its training encourages concise and relevant responses, which explains the straightforward style. Any gaps or incomplete answers may have occurred because it relied on pattern recognition rather than a step-by-step verification of every item before responding.
column25: All answers from the chat bot were simple and direct with, some with a little advice but logical at the end. The chatbot took it by letter and did its job as it was told.
column26: It performed this way because its primary goal is to follow user instructions accurately while maintaining clarity and coherence. Its training emphasizes consistency, directness, and task completion, which is why the responses felt simple, logical, and aligned closely with what was asked.
column27: The main difference I noticed was in depth and completeness. One chatbot tended to give very direct, surface-level answers that focused strictly on what was asked, often without expanding much beyond the prompt. The other appeared more thorough, sometimes adding clarification, extra context, or checking multiple elements before finalizing the response. There was also a difference in accuracy and verification. The simpler chatbot occasionally missed details or did not fully evaluate every item in a list, while the other seemed more careful in reviewing all parts of the question. Overall, one prioritized speed and brevity, and the other emphasized completeness and contextual awareness.

column15:
(I used the same first prompt as the first chatbot, in order to give a fair comparison between the two chatbots.)
 This chatbot correctly identified the bird in the first prompt. However, when asked to create a poem about "it" (the bird), the chatbot responded with a failure message. I rewrote my prompt to make it clearer that I want a poem written about the bird from the list, and the chabot was able to create a poem but it failed to follow the requested AABB rhyme scheme. Even when I asked the chatbot to rewrite the poem, it still responded with the same poem.
column16: I think the chatbot lacks a competent memory about the context/content of the previous conversation turns. It also seems to have less capability in understanding about poem rhyme schemes and doesn't seem to know when the user is requesting for corrections.
column25: The chatbot correctly identified the bird in the first prompt. In later prompts, I referred to the bird as "it" and the chatbot was able to identify that I was referring to the bird from the first response. In the third prompt where I asked the chatbot to create a haiku, it returned a haiku that did not follow the 5-7-5 syllable format. I pointed out the error and the chatbot was able to reason which line was incorrect and corrected itself subsequently.
column26: The chatbot has memory of the context of the previous conversation turns and is able to infer what I am referring to.
column27:
The first chatbot is smarter, has better multi-turn contextual understanding, is able to tell when the user wants its response to be rewritten/corrected and is able to provide an improved response according to user feedback.
 

 The second chatbot is prone to failure, as can be seen from its second response ("Error: Inference tensors cannot be saved for backward. To work around you can make a clone to get a normal tensor and use it in autograd.") It is also unable to self-correct its response in accordance to user feedback. Overall, its response is full of mistakes and is disappointing.
